title: Incursa.Quic Public API Surface
purpose: Define the intended public API surface for Incursa.Quic using the public surface of System.Net.Quic as the baseline for the initial consumer cut, the first honest server-entry slice, and the first honest client-entry slice.
scope: This specification defines the initial consumer-facing public API surface for Incursa.Quic. The repository is implementing the surface in phases, and the current slice promotes the connection-side facade, the first server-side entry surface (`QuicListener`, `QuicListenerOptions`, `QuicServerConnectionOptions`, `ListenAsync`, and `AcceptConnectionAsync`), and the first client-side entry surface (`QuicClientConnectionOptions` and `QuicConnection.ConnectAsync(...)`) over the existing runtime and host machinery. Full TLS trust-store and hostname validation, stream entry points, `QuicStreamCapacityChangedArgs`, `IsSupported`, Initial/DCID bootstrap, and the data path remain deferred. Helper-layer packet, frame, TLS, handshake, runtime, recovery, and congestion types remain internal unless a future specification explicitly promotes them.
context: The reference surface in Microsoft System.Net.Quic is the strongest baseline for the eventual API, but this repository is deliberately shipping that surface in smaller phases. The target repository already contains a broad internal helper and runtime surface under src/Incursa.Quic, and this specification makes the public/internal boundary explicit so the connection-first facade can land without exposing the implementation seams. The client-entry slice is intentionally honest: it promotes the public option bag and connect entry point now, but it does not claim positive connection establishment beyond what the existing runtime and TLS seam can really prove.

requirements.title: Limit the public consumer surface to the approved facade types
requirements.statement: The library MUST expose only the consumer-facing facade types named in this specification, and it MUST keep all other current helper, parser, runtime, TLS, handshake, packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and seam types internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes them.
notes: The approved public facade for the current slice is QuicConnection, QuicStream, QuicConnectionOptions, QuicReceiveWindowSizes, QuicAbortDirection, QuicError, QuicException, QuicListener, QuicListenerOptions, QuicClientConnectionOptions, QuicServerConnectionOptions, and the corrected QuicStreamType.

The current slice reuses the existing runtime, stream-state, listener-host, runtime-endpoint, and endpoint-host machinery directly. The current surface reuses EndPoint, IPEndPoint, SslApplicationProtocol, SslClientAuthenticationOptions, SslServerAuthenticationOptions, and SslClientHelloInfo directly where needed.

requirements.title: Expose the listener and client entry surfaces honestly
requirements.statement: The library MUST expose server listener creation and accept through QuicListener.ListenAsync(QuicListenerOptions, CancellationToken) and QuicListener.AcceptConnectionAsync(CancellationToken), and it MUST expose client connection establishment through QuicConnection.ConnectAsync(QuicClientConnectionOptions, CancellationToken). The client entry point MUST complete only when the existing runtime reaches the supported connection-establishment boundary, and it MUST otherwise remain pending or fail with an explicit terminal outcome instead of fabricating success.
notes: The listener shell is honest and is backed by the internal listener host.
The client shell is honest and is backed by the existing runtime-endpoint and endpoint-host seams over a connected UDP socket.
This slice does not promise a positive client/server loopback path because Initial/DCID bootstrap and listener-side datagram admission are still outside the approved boundary.

requirements.title: Keep the connection model opaque and consumer-facing
requirements.statement: The QuicConnection type MUST represent a connected QUIC session and expose the local endpoint, remote endpoint, target host name, negotiated application protocol, negotiated cipher suite, negotiated TLS protocol, remote certificate, AcceptInboundStreamAsync, OpenOutboundStreamAsync, CloseAsync, DisposeAsync, and ToString, while keeping handshake details, packet state, runtime state, and transport-helper state out of the public surface.
notes: The connection object is the consumer's lifecycle anchor for this slice and may be created only by internal wiring, listener accept flow, or the approved client connect flow.
Transport internals should be accessed through the internal runtime, not through public connection properties.
Connection close, dispose, and terminal-state projection are the only lifecycle behaviors this slice promises.
The connection object is not a middleware or event-pipeline surface.

requirements.title: Keep the stream model aligned with standard .NET stream consumers
requirements.statement: The QuicStream type MUST derive from Stream, expose the stream identifier, stream direction, closed-completion tasks, abort and graceful write-completion operations, and the standard synchronous, asynchronous, span-based, capability, and timeout overrides used by Stream.
notes: The public QuicStreamType enum MUST use the direction-only model with Bidirectional and Unidirectional values.
The richer internal stream-identity model may continue to track initiator detail separately, but it stays internal.

This slice only promises the read-side and terminal-state surface that is backed by QuicConnectionStreamState; write-heavy and abort-heavy stream behavior stays deferred until the stream-action pipeline exists.
The Stream surface stays internal to the facade for now, and the next slice can decide which timeout and write members are honest once stream action exists.

requirements.title: Expose configuration through the approved option and callback types
requirements.statement: The library MUST carry configuration through QuicConnectionOptions, QuicListenerOptions, QuicClientConnectionOptions, QuicServerConnectionOptions, and QuicReceiveWindowSizes, and it MUST expose only the approved knobs on those types: close and stream default error codes, handshake and idle timeouts, keep-alive interval, inbound stream limits, listener endpoint and backlog, client local/remote endpoints, application protocols, the narrow server connection-options callback, server authentication options, and the supported subset of SslClientAuthenticationOptions.
notes: No custom endpoint or handshake wrapper types are introduced for this slice; the listener and client surfaces reuse EndPoint, IPEndPoint, SslApplicationProtocol, SslClientAuthenticationOptions, SslClientHelloInfo, and SslServerAuthenticationOptions directly.
Public option objects are configuration bags, not live mutable protocol state.
Listener setup, client connect setup, and the server callback are now live.

The supported client TLS/auth subset is intentionally narrow: non-empty ALPN, TLS 1.3 or the default protocol selection, no target host / hostname validation, no client certificates or chain policy, an explicit RemoteCertificateValidationCallback gate, and no custom trust, cipher-suite, or renegotiation/resumption overrides. Unsupported SslClientAuthenticationOptions settings MUST be rejected deterministically instead of being silently ignored.
Unsupported SslClientAuthenticationOptions settings MUST be rejected deterministically instead of being silently ignored.
StreamCapacityCallback remains deferred to the follow-on slices.

requirements.title: Keep the close and error surface explicit
requirements.statement: The library MUST classify terminal and shutdown outcomes through QuicError and QuicException, surface application and transport error codes on the exception, and support per-stream abort direction through QuicAbortDirection so consumers can distinguish read, write, and both-side termination without referencing internal protocol state.
notes: Connection shutdown remains available through QuicConnection.CloseAsync(long errorCode, CancellationToken) and DisposeAsync.
The exception surface is the consumer-facing representation of terminal outcomes, not a protocol debug channel.
CloseAsync validates the application error code and throws ArgumentOutOfRangeException for invalid values.
CloseAsync is terminal and repeated close calls are ignored once the connection is already closed.
DisposeAsync is the consumer-facing cleanup path when no explicit close is issued; it uses the configured default close error code.
Stream abort behavior is deferred until the write-side stream-action pipeline exists.

requirements.title: Keep the TLS, handshake, packet, and runtime seams internal
requirements.statement: The library MUST keep QuicTlsTranscriptProgress, QuicTlsKeySchedule, QuicTransportTlsBridgeState, QuicHandshakeFlowCoordinator, QuicInitialPacketProtection, QuicHandshakePacketProtection, QuicTlsPacketProtectionMaterial, QuicConnectionRuntime, QuicConnectionRuntimeHost, QuicListenerHost, QuicConnectionRuntimeShard, QuicConnectionSendRuntime, QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint, QuicConnectionEndpointHost, QuicClientConnectionHost, and QuicClientConnectionOptionsValidator internal, and it MUST keep the remainder of the packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and stream-identity helpers internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes them.
notes: This boundary keeps listener-host, client-host, runtime-endpoint, endpoint-host, validator, transcript progress, key schedule, bridge state, handshake coordination, packet-protection details, runtime reducer details, stream-state helpers, packet helpers, and transport-parameter helpers out of the consumer contract.
The public API must not expose the existing helper-layer runtime model by accident.

requirements.title: Honor async lifecycle cancellation and terminal-state transitions
requirements.statement: The library MUST honor cancellation tokens on pending listen, accept, connect, open-stream, and close operations, and the server connection-options callback MUST observe the cancellation token supplied by the listener accept flow. A pre-canceled or later-canceled token MUST complete the still-pending operation with OperationCanceledException, but once a listener accept, connection, or stream has committed or reached a terminal state the public API MUST complete with the documented QuicError or ObjectDisposedException outcome instead of silently reviving or hiding the operation.
notes: The Microsoft tests directly prove cancellation for listener accept, connect, the listener callback, and blocked outbound stream opens; this slice applies that evidence to the listener path and to the still-pending client connect path.
Cancellation does not retroactively undo a committed connection, accepted stream, or completed close.
Pending connect cancellation and disposal must not fabricate handshake completion.
Idempotent close and dispose semantics are consumer-facing terminal behavior, not a hidden runtime optimization.

supplemental_sections.heading: Public Surface Inventory
supplemental_sections.content:
- `QuicListener`: server-side listen/accept entry point.
- `QuicConnection`: connected session facade and client connect entry point.
- `QuicStream`: standard `Stream` facade for a QUIC stream.
- `QuicConnectionOptions`: shared connection knobs.
- `QuicListenerOptions`: listener configuration and narrow server connection-options callback.
- `QuicClientConnectionOptions`: client-side connect configuration.
- `QuicServerConnectionOptions`: server-side accept options.
- `QuicReceiveWindowSizes`: connection and stream receive-window settings.
- `QuicAbortDirection`: stream abort direction.
- `QuicError` and `QuicException`: close and terminal error surface.
- `QuicStreamType`: bidirectional/unidirectional stream direction.
- `QuicStreamCapacityChangedArgs`: still deferred to the stream-capacity slice.

The public API should reuse `EndPoint`, `IPEndPoint`, `SslApplicationProtocol`, `SslClientAuthenticationOptions`, `SslClientHelloInfo`, and `SslServerAuthenticationOptions` directly rather than introduce new endpoint or TLS wrapper abstractions.

supplemental_sections.heading: Behavioral Evidence From Microsoft Tests
supplemental_sections.content:
The following Microsoft library-focused tests were the behavioral baseline for this slice:

- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicListenerTests.cs`: listener setup validation, callback exception and timeout behavior, cancellation, backlog, ALPN, and listener-disposal behavior.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicConnectionTests.cs`: connect validation, close/dispose terminal-state behavior, stream-capacity callback deltas, peer-certificate lifetime, and peer-abort propagation.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicStreamTests.cs`: direction-based capabilities, EOF and graceful completion, abort mapping, completion-task behavior, cancellation, and stream-dispose behavior.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/QuicStreamConnectedStreamConformanceTests.cs`: `QuicStream` participates in the standard timeout-aware `Stream` contract.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/MsQuicCipherSuitesPolicyTests.cs`: the API reuses BCL TLS options directly and surfaces invalid cipher-suite combinations through public exceptions.
- `tests/FunctionalTests/MsQuicTests.cs`: idle timeout, blocked outbound stream open cancellation, and idempotent close behavior.

These tests justified the listener validation, accept cancellation/disposal, and callback-token language in `REQ-QUIC-API-0002`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0005`, and `REQ-QUIC-API-0008`, while also continuing to justify the lifecycle and cancellation language in `REQ-QUIC-API-0003`, `REQ-QUIC-API-0004`, and `REQ-QUIC-API-0006`.

supplemental_sections.heading: Internal-Only Surface
supplemental_sections.content:
The current helper and runtime seam types stay internal, including `QuicTlsTranscriptProgress`, `QuicTlsKeySchedule`, `QuicTransportTlsBridgeState`, `QuicHandshakeFlowCoordinator`, `QuicInitialPacketProtection`, `QuicHandshakePacketProtection`, `QuicTlsPacketProtectionMaterial`, `QuicConnectionRuntime`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeHost`, `QuicListenerHost`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeShard`, `QuicConnectionSendRuntime`, `QuicConnectionRuntimeEndpoint`, `QuicConnectionEndpointHost`, `QuicClientConnectionHost`, and `QuicClientConnectionOptionsValidator`.

The remainder of the packet, frame, transport-parameter, recovery, congestion, and stream-identity helpers also remain internal unless a future requirement explicitly promotes one of them.

supplemental_sections.heading: Deviation Policy
supplemental_sections.content:
No intentional deviations from the Microsoft ref surface are required for this slice.

The repo-specific rule is that the richer internal transport engine stays hidden behind the consumer facade, the supported client TLS/auth subset is explicit and reject-first, and the public surface does not grow into a general middleware model.
